finding: dedup_tool_results_by_id dedups tool_use_ids across the WHOLE request, but ids are only unique within one assistant message (resolve_unique_tool_use_id seeds `used` from WHERE message_id = $1 and deliberately tolerates gpt-oss/harmony's constant "tool_use"), so a provider reusing an id across turns has its later turn's legitimate result dropped and its tool_use left unpaired

replace_or_collect_tool_results' find_map scans the entire request history, so with a cross-turn id collision it overwrites an OLDER turn's result (corrupting history) and returns no leftover, leaving the CURRENT turn's tool_use unanswered

The approval claim discards delete_tool_approval's Ok(bool) (rows_affected > 0) and branches only on Err, so Ok(false) — the row already claimed by a concurrent pass — is treated as a successful claim and the tool executes anyway; the claim is not exactly-once

The claim-failure arm pushed an is_error tool_result and continued while leaving the approval row intact, so the same turn's after_llm_call re-fetched and executed it for real — persisting a SECOND tool_result for that tool_use_id, the exact duplicate this feature exists to prevent

approved_tool_is_claimed_and_executes_exactly_once would still pass with the claim moved back after execution — pre-fix the post-execution DELETE succeeded on this happy path, so all assertions hold either way; the bug needs a losing/failing DELETE the test never induces

results_by_id.clear() only evicts orphans captured BEFORE a flush; an orphan in a batch with no prior flush ([result X stale, use X, result X real]) still shadows the later real result via keep-first, so the hazard is only half-closed and TEST-5 exercises only the covered half

The doc test string-matches the arbitrary literal "is the next step", so any paraphrase of the same stale claim passes; the cited wsl2.rs precedent scans for a security-relevant CODE pattern, not prose, so it does not support prose-linting
dedup_tool_results_is_a_noop_on_a_valid_request claims the request is byte-identical but only compares role and content.len(); block contents are never compared though ChatMessage derives Serialize

resume_shape_keeps_exactly_one_result_per_tool_use asserts only shape/count, so it silently certifies an outcome where keep-first discards B's REAL result and leaves the synthesized is_error placeholder as the surviving answer

The model-facing skip result leaks internal jargon ('approval record', 'claimed'), omits the tool name and the underlying error that sibling arms include, gives no next step, and leaves the model unable to tell whether the tool ran

The claim-failure arm is the only error arm that does not push to executed_tool_use_ids, so the id reaches after_llm_call only via the persisted-tool_result fallback — the write most likely to have failed in exactly the DB-trouble scenario that made the claim fail
assert!(collide.is_err()) accepts ANY error as proof the unique constraint fired, unlike the suite's existing idiom (tests/llm_model/download_test.rs) which asserts db.is_unique_violation()
The catch_unwind control swaps the PROCESS-GLOBAL panic hook, so under cargo test's parallel threads it would swallow panic output from any genuinely failing concurrent test

The warn! is framed as a one-shot tripwire but sits on a path rebuilt every agentic iteration, so a persistent duplicate source emits one warn per block per iteration, with no conversation_id/message_id to correlate the request it is meant to diagnose
delete_tool_approval's doc still reads 'Delete tool use approval record (after execution)' although its primary caller now invokes it BEFORE execution as a claim
The claim-failure arm omits the executed_tool_use_ids.push that every sibling error arm performs
register_http_mcp/db_pool/send_body/start_echo_mock are near-verbatim copies of the identically-named helpers in tests/mcp/mcp_approval_loop_test.rs rather than promoted to a shared fixtures module
dedup_tool_results_by_id clones every tool_use_id into a HashSet on every provider request
Migration 158 drops an index — checked for authz/isolation/regen implications

All 7 PLAN items reconciled against shipped code with file:line evidence (ITEM-1 mcp.rs:358+1610, ITEM-2 streaming.rs:468+1844, ITEM-3 streaming.rs:1714, ITEM-4 mcp.rs:708, ITEM-5 contents.rs:54, ITEM-6 migration 158, ITEM-7 lib.rs:310); no item absent, no unapproved descope

ROUND 2: ClaimOutcome::Failed => return Err(e) aborts mid-loop and discards the tool_results of every approval EARLIER in the same batch that already won its claim and executed — their side effects happened, their rows are gone, their results are never persisted, leaving those tool_uses permanently unanswerable

ROUND 2: DEC-4's stated mitigation ('the tool_use gets a synthesized is_error placeholder on the next turn — degraded but valid') is FALSE for a batch where nothing ran (the single-tool approval, i.e. the common case): group_assistant_blocks' batch_has_result gate emits a BARE Assistant turn with no Tool message, so the tool_use stays unpaired on every subsequent request and the branch is bricked

ROUND 2: the AlreadyClaimed skip pushed neither a tool_result nor an executed_tool_use_ids entry, so a single-tool batch is left with no result and no approval row — unrecoverable, since the provider rejects the unpaired request before after_llm_call can ever run

ROUND 2: batch_start = rposition(...).unwrap_or(0) inverts the function's own SCOPE rule when no Assistant-with-tool_use exists — it searches the WHOLE request; unwrap_or(messages.len()) is the fail-safe 'search nothing' default

ROUND 2: results_by_id.clear() is dead code — the new capture guard makes the map's keys a subset of current_tool_uses' ids, all of which the flush loop already drains via remove(), so the map is provably empty when clear() runs

ROUND 2: TEST-5's doc claims to cover the results_by_id.clear() half, but its fixture is already handled by the capture guard alone, so it passes with clear() deleted and does not discriminate what it documents
ROUND 2: TEST-10's file header overclaims ('drives the REAL path', 'the decisive assertions') for a test that passes pre-fix — it is a regression guard; TEST-13 is the discriminating leg

ROUND 2 re-audit: dedup_tool_results_by_id turn-group scoping re-verified against counterexamples (text-only Assistant, results before any Assistant, gpt-oss cross-turn reuse, the Assistant/Tool/User bug shape) — reset lands at the correct boundary; emptied/retain index arithmetic sound

ROUND 2 re-audit: group_assistant_blocks keep-first re-verified after the capture-guard change (use A, result A, result A-dup with and without an intervening flush) — the duplicate is still dropped and batch_has_result still reads correctly

ROUND 2 re-audit: return Err from the approval loop leaks nothing — the spawned elicitation task is anchored to elicit_notify_rx and ends when the tx drops at function exit; _guard/_owned are per-iteration bindings declared after the claim
ROUND 2: flush_assistant_tool_pair's doc still states orphan results 'are left in results_by_id (dropped by the caller)', contradicted by the capture guard that never inserts an orphan at all

ROUND 2: the doc test claims 'a rename or a removal of the real guard fails here', but a future DROP CONSTRAINT would leave it green since migration 124's ADD CONSTRAINT text still matches — it proves a migration once added the name, not that the guard survives

ROUND 2: claim_outcome_distinguishes_won_already_claimed_and_failed exercises the pure helper in isolation, so reverting the call site to the bool-discarding if-let-Err form leaves it green despite its doc claiming it discriminates the fix

ROUND 2: the integration test queries JOIN messages m ... WHERE m.branch_id, but messages has no branch_id column (branch ownership is via the branch_messages junction table) — sqlx::query_scalar is not compile-checked, so it built fine and panicked at runtime

ROUND 3: ClaimOutcome::AlreadyClaimed's doc says 'Skip WITHOUT emitting anything', but the handler pushes an is_error result and continues — the enum's contract now argues FOR the bricked-branch hazard round 2 closed
ROUND 3: ClaimOutcome::Failed's doc says 'fail the turn loudly', but no path fails the turn — the handler pushes an is_error result and continues, so the variant doc misdescribes its own handler

ROUND 3: the comment claims 'the real result is deduped keep-first at assembly', but on AlreadyClaimed this pass persists its is_error result while the winner is still executing, so the error takes the lower sequence_order and keep-first makes it permanently authoritative

ROUND 3: dedup_tool_results_by_id's summary line and the caller's warn! claim it enforces one result per tool_use_id 'across the whole request', which the seen.clear() turn-group scoping deliberately narrows — the headline overclaims what its own SCOPE paragraph corrects
ROUND 3: TEST-18 asserts unchanged pre-existing behavior and passes with the whole diff reverted — it documents a hazard rather than guarding a change, and its doc did not say so

ROUND 3 claim that NO test fails with the claim reordering reverted — REFUTED BY RUNNING IT: a revert-probe (claim disabled at the loop head, delete restored after execution) makes mcp_approval_loop_unresolvable_tool_errors_and_terminates FAIL, because that arm continues BEFORE execution and this diff removed its private delete, so the row only disappears if the claim precedes execution. The reordering IS discriminated end-to-end

ROUND 3 re-audit: traced all six exits of the approval loop — every one pushes exactly one tool_results entry and one executed_tool_use_ids entry; no path continues without pushing; no ? inside the loop body; no tool_use left unpaired

ROUND 3 re-audit: proved the debug_assert!(results_by_id.is_empty()) cannot fire — a key enters the map only via pending_ids.remove()==true, whose id was pushed to current_tool_uses, which only flush drains by removing every element's id; keep-first also still holds since insert can never overwrite. Verified live: 319 chat+mcp tests pass in a debug build
ROUND 3 re-audit: messages[batch_start..] with batch_start == messages.len() yields an empty slice, not a panic; batch_start is computed once and no message is inserted/removed in the loop

ROUND 3 re-audit: ran the integration test's pg_index query against a real migrated build DB (_sqlx_migrations version 158 present) — returns exactly uq_message_contents_message_sequence; unnest(x.indkey) verified working (int2vector binary-coercible to int2[])

ROUND 4: the Failed path's model-facing copy claims 'It was not executed twice' and the comment scopes the stale-error-wins cost to AlreadyClaimed ('needs two concurrent passes'), but a failed DELETE leaves the row at status='approved' so after_llm_call's get_approved_tools_for_branch re-claims and executes it later in the SAME request — one pass, one flaky DELETE reaches the identical cost

ROUND 4: append_content's new doc asserts a retry 'would be dead code guarding a call shape that does not exist', but the MCP extension's detached elicitation task calls append_content_with_id (same MAX+1-inside-INSERT, same message_id) concurrently with the approval loop's append_content calls, and that site swallows the unique violation with `let _ =` — so the promised loud failure is silent there

ROUND 4 re-audit: dedup SCOPE doc matches code exactly; the reset is strictly finer-grained than resolve_unique_tool_use_id's per-message_id guarantee so it cannot false-drop; a Tool turn is always preceded by an Assistant-with-tool_use that clears seen, so it is never emptied/stranded; ordering vs clear_old_tool_results verified

ROUND 4 re-audit: the 'no clear() needed, map is provably empty' argument re-verified airtight (captured keys ⊆ pending_ids ⊆ current_tool_uses ids, all removed by the flush loop); TEST-5/TEST-5b both confirmed to genuinely fail on base
ROUND 4 re-audit: found NO test whose assertions are narrower than its claim — TEST-18/TEST-13/approval_claim_test/TEST-9 all state their honest scope explicitly

ROUND 4 prose note: migration 158 + TEST-11/12 + the append_content doc rewrite are a redundant-index cleanup unrelated to the duplicate-tool_result bug, adding DDL deploy risk to a bugfix branch — worth a conscious keep/split decision
